narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 24, Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate strong. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were, in order of influence, your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA. Your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward a failure prediction, as it stands notably below the typical score of around 37 seen in the dataset, signaling to the model a potential gap in the standardized reasoning skills needed for bar success. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 2—much lower than the average of 5.5—strongly reinforced the failure outlook by indicating relatively weaker performance compared to peers in your school that year. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, while below the average of 3.21, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in your college record. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your metrics in these key areas are lower, placing you outside the stronger profiles the model favors for admission. Your full-time status aligns with most applicants at 93%, so it didn't sway things much, nor did your middle income bracket. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with drive and background—many overcome similar starts—but the model weighs these academic signals heavily for bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your upper-year grades if possible, and perhaps seeking targeted prep courses or academic advising to build a profile that reassures admissions teams. With focused improvements, you can reapply stronger and shift the odds in your favor.
